author: PriorResult9949
text:
You have become a prisoner. This dude is abusive and manipulative. The reason shit got so toxic at your parents house? Is because they are adults and saw right thru him. You are still very young and I’m sure you absolutely loveeeeeeee him and would hear none of it. 

Your boyfriend used this to his advantage and stirred the shit pot even more to get you on his side and isolate you from your folks.

But he won’t stop there. Trust me. 

That shit about not trusting you from looking out the fucking window ? Here is a real life example. 

One time I was dating this toxic and abusive guy. The true colors had started to fade after a few weeks. I was at the store in the check out line. I leaned into give him a kiss. And he pulled back and pushed be back and started a fight with me at the register about looking at some man that walked behind him while I was kissing him who I never even saw in the first place. Which was all bullshit and became an all night war. Stiff was broken . 

Does that sound rational or fair? Because that is the path you’re on.

Keeping you locked in?? Telling you to quit working? Not letting you decorate ? Assuming you’re looking at people or men when you’re not? Soon it’s gonna be “ why are you wearing makeup? Why are you wearing a nice shirt? Why are you talking to your parents? They want you to marry someone else! You can’t talk to your friends anymore they just want to fuck you or set you up with someone else!”

It’s gonna be anything and everything. Only his friends can come over and he will accuse you of flirting with them.

It knit gets worse. He will beat your ass. Take all your money and never let you out or back in the apartment he conned you to move into with him. 

You are a prisoner. You’re his sex slave. And whenever you’re not in the mood? He will accuse you of talking to someone else. Your phone is gonna get destroyed at some point. He goes thru it no doubt. But he will eventually destroy it because he doesn’t want you to have any line to call for help.

He is sick. And you deserve better. 

You better get away from him and you will likely have to get a restraining order because men like that don’t take rejection without handing out punishment.


I’m not projecting on you. Just because I’ve had my share of this. Is there a chance he could be saved and ch age his way? Sure. But I doubt it.

You’re asking for advice. And this is it.

Get as far away from him as possible. Go home. Talk to your family and I’m sure they will understand. No matter how difficult things got. They saw what he is and probably tried to warn you because they give a shit about you. Your parents don’t want to have to identify your brutally beaten body at a morgue because this guy has the hallmarks of a violent abusive partner. But because you’re an adult? They can’t tell you shit and you didn’t want to hear it anyway.

Go home. Take all your shit that you can carry and leave. If you value your sanity, and your freedom you better do it as soon as possible. No more excuses for this man!! 

You really ought to seek therapy. There is damage already done and you have been ignoring red flags thru this whole thing. 

Men/ women like this are master manipulators and sell us a version of themselves to us that they know we will fall in love and defend to maintain this false illusion. We will fight and burn every bridge to maintain this narrative that they have created. 

Once you start to see the lies and ask questions and god forbid stand up to them for yourself? They lose their shit because they lose control. This is why he is making up shit about not letting you have a house key and wanting to keep you locked in. 

It’s absolutely abuse. And you have become a prisoner. Don’t you ever forget that. He will want you to get pregnant as soon as he can to maintain having control. It’s not about the joy of marriage and starting a family. 

This is cruelty. You’re a hostage without even realizing what it’s happening. None of this is normal behavior..

author: Spiley_spile
text:
That sounds very stressful. Im sorry you are on different pages about the pregnancy. Children are a huge, life altering addition. Whether you help raise them or pay child support for the rest of their lives.

The train appears to have left the station on this one. I can't tell you the number of people Ive met who, for whatever reason, dont learn from the first time this happens. So, Ill spell it out and hopefully, you dont put yourself in this position again.

The time to decide you arent ready to have kids is before you take the risk of getting pregnant together. There are things both people can do to protect themselves. I dont recommend having only 1 person taking preventative measures. Both people have options they can implemement to help prevent pregnancy:

Condoms, birth control, vasectomies. (Vasectomy is reversable and very short healing time), and hysterectomies, uterectomies, or bisalpingectomies. (Not reversable, longer healing times, and usually more difficult to get doctor/insurance approval for).

author: bombshellbrat

If this is a new kind of pain than youre used to, be sure to schedule an appointment with a professional for a full physical.  Dont ignore extreme pain, especially during your "lady time"  get it checked out and make sure there is nothing extra going on.   If you are in a place where you can find Cannabis suppositories for PMS you could try that,  some places make medicated bath bombs that also work really well.  Edibles would also be a solid choice.  Good luck and remember, you are not alone.. this pain SUCKS.

author: friendlyfire69
text:
A lot of dandruff is caused by fungal overgrowth. Hormone changes with pregnancy can make you more prone to yeast. Hair oil can feed yeast. I recommend adding tea tree oil into your oil mix as an antifungal if you like using hair oil.

Nizoral antifungal shampoo may be a good choice. It has ketoconazole. Also could try a salicylic acid shampoo like T-Sal to exfoliate dead skin.

author: sphorx13
text: Much of the food you eat can be quite filling and doesnt contain many calories. Assuming you already have a small appetite, eating filling foods with low calorie density is not going to sustain your calorific needs as its unlikely you are able to eat enough of it. Without sounding patronising jt really is a simple equation of calorie input vs energy output. Your body works the same way as everyone elses and if you put more calories in than what you use, you will gain weight until you reach an equillibrium. Find foods you enjoy and eat plenty of them. Ideally high calorie foods though, not veg and rice and tofu. Cheese, cream, meat, bread, fat etc. I used to be a bit underweight when i was younger and i thought i was eating the same as everyone else but now im hindsight realise i definitely wasnt. Other people eat more than they let on or realise. As soon as you start consistently eating good calorie meals and snacking in between youll fill right out. Have bigger portions, make sure you have a desert after dinner, be more generous with sauces and dips and sides etc. All easy ways to bulk up a bit. A bit of fast food wont kill you either. If your going on walks or runs youll need even more to match what youre burning off. Really depends how much weight you want to gain and keeping an eye on it. Youre not going to balloon up overnight so if you start getting where you want to be you ca. Adjust it down a bit again until you find the right levels.

author: GreenSpaniel
text:
You are absolutely not to blame for the actions of someone else.   
   
As for your moral rule, I think that it's a good principle to not talk to a third person about your relationships, however, you may need some flexibility. In this instance, you were absolutely correct not to share information, it is not up to you to build trust in someone else's relationship, you did the right thing. However, you might wish to consider how this rule might affect your future relationships. Not necessarily divulging details, but there are things that people like to know, such as if you are a virgin or how many sexual partners you've had, purely from a health point of view, or for understanding if you're nervous, etc. So you may want to briefly talk about past relationships in your future relationships, however, you wouldn't need to provide details about the people.

author: Business-Gazelle-203
text: I know you’re panicking right now, but making yourself sick on purpose is honestly dangerous and not the way out of this. That feeling of “I can’t do this, I need to be ill” is just your brain going into overload because you’re stressed, not because you actually need to harm yourself. The safest thing you can do is be honest about not being prepared or not being able to do whatever this thing is tomorrow, rather than hurting yourself. Forcing yourself to vomit or raise your temperature can cause real damage, and you don’t deserve that at all. If whatever’s happening tomorrow feels unsafe or overwhelming, please talk to someone you trust, and if you’re feeling trapped or like you might hurt yourself, contact someone who can help right now, like Samaritans on 116 123 in the UK or your local crisis line. You’re not weak, you’re overwhelmed, and you don’t have to harm yourself to get out of this x

author: Selorn041
text:
Ask yourself this, say you spend 6 months, maybe a year in therapy. Your wife seems to be doing better, has addressed her underlying issue that is causing her behavior. 

Can you ever really see yourself trusting her again? Like really trusting her? Can you see yourself forgiving her for her transgressions? Not harboring that anger, resentment, suspicion that will eat you from the inside until it destroys your relationship anyways, can you see yourself letting that go?

I ask because you said he held onto your suspicions after the first time, lack of trust, and hadn't really forgiven her for 2 years (you ended up being right, but you didn't know that for sure for the 2 years leading up to this incident)

If you can't, then don't waste the time going to therapy to try to fix something that can't be fixed, sometimes relationships are broken beyond repair. Living in a house with that tension, isn't going to do you or your children any good in the long run. Instead take the money you would have spent on couples therapy and spend it on co-parenting therapy. 

I know you mentioned the material things, and it is devastating to loose those things or even half of those things, but things can be replaced. The time you spend here is finite, spending that time in a toxic relationship isn't how I would want to spend the precious time that I have been given.

author: Emdeedee123
text:
Don’t stress too much. It happens to the best of us.  Especially when we’re new drivers.  Your mother lent you her car knowing you’re a new driver so she understood the risk. 

But mums be mums, and sometimes their initial reaction is to get upset.  But honestly, getting upset at a new driver for scratching a car is a bit like getting upset at a kid learning to ride a bike for catching the bike.  

Be respectful if she does get upset though.  Not necessarily because you’ve marked her property, but kids getting their license is a terrifying time for parents.  

As for the other car, what happens really depends on how bad the damage is, and what type of person or what mood the person is in when they see your note.

Some people will not even worry about following anything up and just appreciate that someone had the courtesy to let them know.  Others will try to make you responsible for an entire repaint. 

In future, ALWAYS take a photo or video footage of where you hit or think you hit a car.  Even if you didn’t do any damage.  

Don’t let this weigh too heavily.  It’s just a scratch on a car.

I wish you a lifetime of safe driving.

author: Winter-Rice2811
text:
I’m sorry you went through that traumatic time. 

I’m going to say something kind of rough in the hopes that in the long term it helps, so sorry in advance, but you need to stop holding on to this relationship. She was potentially being considerate by not blocking you, but she didn’t respond to your message. She’s showing you that she doesn’t want to feed into your hope of a reconciliation by not engaging in a convo with you. 

Personal growth takes time- more than a couple months. I personally wouldn’t believe someone has actually changed in that space of time. You’re clearly making the steps, which is amazing, but don’t rush things. 
I also would not send that letter. Be mentally well and healthy for you, not because you think it’ll win her back.

author: SOURSKOOMA
text:
You can try to spice it up with some new things.

Maybe pick up a musical instrument. Get a teacher too. That will force you to get out & have some interactions outside of work and home. Might even make a friend or two along the way.

It seems like your life is kinda out of your control for the most part. You gotta find some things to implement in your life that you can control & really lean into them. This is a big part of why prisoners work out quite a bit. They have basically no control of their lives, but their muscles and mind are something that "usually" can't be controlled by others. So, you could try working out as well. Maybe get a trainer or make a friend at the gym or whatever sport you decide to participate in.

You're also 19, so you could move out if you really wanted to (though I do know that it's not the most ideal thing to do nowadays... financially speaking). Maybe if you networked & tried to build solid connections with your co-workers, you might be able to split rent on a decent house, which would separate you from the family dynamic. My family treated me like Cinderella until I left & realized how good they had it with me as their slave/bitch...they became a lot more respectful not long after.

Wish you luck, I've been there before and it totally blows.

author: draconicmonkey
text:
In life you have to make compromises and sacrifices - you can choose what compromises and sacrifices you are willing to make, but you can’t choose whether you have to make them or not. Time naturally does the selection of you don’t, typically by limiting your available options.

So knowing that and the fact that you have a single life to live and you have to live with the outcomes of those choices - my advice is to make sure you are choosing wisely between what others want of you and what you want for yourself. Weigh the importance of your happy and peaceful home vs a happy and healthy relationship with family. It is not an ideal place to have to compromise but one that you should make deliberately and with your feelings carefully weighed.

Personally I would follow my heart and date/marry whomever I want - because a lifetime is a terrible thing to waste sharing it with someone you’re not in love with and who doesn’t treat you well.

author: allencb
text:
I'm 52 and in the US, so my experiences will be a bit different than yours.  I was a business major in college, enrolled in a program that enabled me to earn my BBA and MBA in 5 years (by using masters level courses as undergrad electives and taking a very heavy course load most of the time I was there).  Keep in mind the computer industry was very different then than now.  The Internet had not yet become commonly available and "working with computers" meant Cobol programming, mainframes and minicomputers, and nothing like the industry it is now.  While I had an interest in computers, such as they were at the time, the idea of being a Cobol programmer or working on a mainframe didn't appeal to me despite me having "the knack".  Engineering would have been a good choice, but the only engineer I knew in high school was unemployed, while my business major dad had been gainfully employed my entire life.  Therefore, I would be a business major.  My interest was marketing, and I ended up getting a minor in Marketing.  

After school, I landed a job at Perot Systems (a tech services company founded by Ross Perot) and spent a couple years floating among short-term roles of various types until I landed a spot on a contract managing a large development network where I was solely responsible for over 200 WinNT workstations and several servers (initially WinNT as well, but I brought in some Linux systems).  This was a time when anyone with any ability in IT could find a new job at a drop of the hat.  Many of my colleagues had all sorts of unrelated degrees (including Geology for one and a GED with some community college for another).

After that, I left and went to work for GTE, who later became Verizon, as a network engineer tasked with managing a VPN service for a large govt contract.  I spent 10 years there and got experience not only in the technical operations but also as an unofficial sales engineer for that service, meeting with Govt decision makers who were interested in buying our service.  I also jumped on opportunities to get involved with activities not aligned to the VPN service I was responsible for, expanding my skills.

When that project came to an end, I landed a role at BT (yes, that BT) working in their managed security division in a role that most companies call a Technical Account Manager.  Over the 15 years I've been there, I've managed teams of engineers and eventually one of our US SOCs.  I'm now managing a number of cybersecurity-aligned teams, mostly based in the UK and Europe.  In the meantime, I've also earned a number of certifications (CISSP, CISM, ITIL, etc).

All that to say while I have a business degree without any sort of formal CS or IT degree, I've had a long career in that industry.  The degree matters (or at least back then) less than your ability and drive, it simply opens a door.  I've found my degrees useful as a manager and service lead.  For example, when I had to build a financial model for the VPN service showing what level of sales we would need to be profitable, what I learned in school suddenly became relevant.

If CS isn't your interest, go for the business degree.  It offers a lot of flexibility as long as you can demonstrate other skills that may be useful to companies.  That said, starting salaries for business roles are lower than starting salaries in the CS/IT/Cyber space.  I'll also point out that many roles in the IT and Cyber realm are starting to ask for CS degrees, so if you want to get into IT or Cyber, the CS degree might give you an advantage until you gain some experience.   

Ok, that was a wall of text.  I hope it helps. :)

author: Trumystic6791
text:
OP you need to wake up and think of your own timelines completely removed from your boyfriend.  Where do you see yourself in 2027 and what do you want to be doing? Where do you see yourself by 2030 and what do you want to be doing? Good now you have clarity on what *you* want. Someone who loves you and cares for you puts you first and considers you in the day to day decisions as well as larger life decisions. Someone you care for will also be willing to come together and craft choices together that make both of you happy. You need to assess if your boyfriend is the type of person who is considerate of you and if he is someone you can build with. Based on what youve said it doesnt seem like he is.

When you are calm let him know that you feel not including you in holiday plans makes you feel like you are not a priority in his life. Then see what he says.

At another time perhaps right after Christmas sit down with your boyfriend and ask him what his plans are regarding marriage with you. Dont ask for his intentions as thats too vague. Ask him what his plans and timelines are for those same time periods( by 2027/by2030) and further ask him what are the concrete actions he will take that demonstrate he is moving forward on those plans. Ask him directly if he plans on marrying you. If he pauses, gets tongue tied or very evasive about applying those concrete plans to you well then you have your answer. Break up with him and find someone who will love you, put you first and is willing to make a commitment to you.

author: WoodedSpys
text:
what she really means is that she us furious that you moved away and are no longer her punching bag so she is lashing out because its the only thing she knows how to do. She is saying that you are mean in an effort to guilt trip you into continuing to be her punching bag. Dont let her guilt you, she is abusive. No one, let alone your mother, should call you terrible things like 'ungrateful' and 'not enough'. Your mother is emotionally abusive and getting away from her will be one of the best things you do for yourself. 

By punching bag, I mean the item that she can emotionally hit, kick, and take out all her anger on, an inanimate object that she doesnt not care about. She does not care if the bag (you) it gets ripped or dirty, or damaged. 

To be clear, I would cut all contact with her and block her number, I also wouldnt tell her where you live now. This is the advice I would give anyone trying to get away from an abusive relative.
